questions.question: Explain the difference between &arr and arr where arr is an integer array.
keyPoints: &arr gives the address of the entire array (type: int (*)[n])
arr gives the address of the first element (type: int*)
Both have the same numeric value but different pointer types
&arr points to the whole array, arr points to first element only

questions.question: What is a dangling pointer? How can it be avoided?
keyPoints: Dangling pointer points to freed/deallocated memory
It occurs when memory is freed but pointer still references that location
Avoid by: initializing pointer to NULL after free, using pointers only within scope
Always free memory after use and set pointer to NULL

questions.question: Explain pointer arithmetic with an example.
keyPoints: Pointer arithmetic allows adding/subtracting integers to pointer addresses
Adding 1 to int* moves by sizeof(int) bytes
Subtracting two pointers gives the number of elements between them
Only subtraction of two pointers from same array is valid

questions.question: What is the difference between int *ptr and const int *ptr?
keyPoints: int *ptr - pointer can change address, can modify value through dereferencing
const int *ptr - pointer can change address, CANNOT modify value through this pointer
First form: regular pointer to int
Second form: pointer to constant int (read-only through this pointer)

questions.question: Explain the relationship between arrays and pointers in C.
keyPoints: Array name acts as a constant pointer to the first element
arr[i] is equivalent to *(arr + i)
Array name cannot be reassigned (constant)
Passing array to function passes pointer to first element

questions.question: What is a NULL pointer? Explain its importance.
keyPoints: NULL pointer stores value 0 (zero), representing no valid address
Used to indicate pointer doesn't point to any object
Dereferencing NULL causes undefined behavior (usually crash)
Always initialize pointers to NULL when not assigned

questions.question: What is a void pointer? What are its limitations?
keyPoints: void* is a generic pointer that can point to any data type
Cannot be directly dereferenced - must be cast to specific type first
Pointer arithmetic not allowed on void pointers (until C99)
Used for generic memory handling functions like malloc

questions.question: Write a C program to dynamically allocate memory for an array of n integers using malloc(), fill it with values, and then free the memory. Explain each step.
keyPoints: Use malloc(n * sizeof(int)) to allocate memory
Check if malloc returned NULL (allocation failure)
Use malloc return value to fill array elements
Use free() to release allocated memory
Explain pointer usage and memory management

questions.question: Explain the concept of pointer to pointer (double pointer) with a C program example showing how it can be used to modify a pointer inside a function.
keyPoints: Double pointer stores address of another pointer
Used to modify pointer value from inside a function
Demonstrate with function that changes original pointer
Explain how **ptr dereferences to get final value
Show practical use in dynamic memory allocation

questions.question: Write a C program using pointers to reverse an array. Explain how pointer arithmetic helps in array traversal.
keyPoints: Use two pointers: one at start, one at end
Swap values using pointers dereferencing
Move pointers toward center until they meet
Explain how *(arr+i) and *(arr+j) access elements
Demonstrate pointer-based array manipulation

questions.question: Explain the different types of const pointers in C with examples: const int *p, int *const p, and const int *const p.
keyPoints: const int *p - pointer to constant: can change p, cannot change *p
int *const p - constant pointer: cannot change p, can change *p
const int *const p - constant pointer to constant: cannot change either
Explain initialization and modification rules for each
